reference: GATE CS — Theory of Computation / Automata

map.condition: Need to test if a CFG generates any string (non-emptiness)
map.use: \text{Emptiness of CFL is decidable; check if start symbol derives a terminal string}

map.condition: DPDA vs NPDA — which language class?
Language accepted by DPDA (empty stack vs final state)?
map.use: \text{DPDA (final state)} \supsetneq \text{DPDA (empty stack)}; \text{ every regular language is DCFL}
\text{Emptiness, finiteness, membership decidable; Equivalence, ambiguity undecidable for CFG}

map.condition: PDA accepts palindromes or matched brackets?
map.use: \{ww^R \mid w \in \Sigma^*\} \text{ is DCFL}; \text{ matched brackets } \{a^n b^n\} \text{ is DCFL}

formulas.name: Context-Free Grammar (formal definition)
formulas.latex: G = (V, \Sigma, R, S) \text{ where } V = \text{variables}, \; \Sigma = \text{terminals}, \; R \subseteq V \times (V \cup \Sigma)^*, \; S \in V
formulas.plain: G = (V, Sigma, R, S): V = non-terminals, Sigma = terminals, R = production rules, S = start variable
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever formally defining or identifying components of a CFG
formulas.trap: Confusing variables with terminals; the start symbol S must be in V, not Sigma

formulas.name: Language generated by a CFG
formulas.latex: L(G) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* \mid S \xRightarrow{*} w \}
formulas.plain: L(G) = { w in Sigma* | S derives w in zero or more steps }
formulas.whenToUse: Determining what strings belong to the language of a grammar

terms.means: derives in zero or more steps

formulas.trap: Derivation must end in ALL terminals; intermediate steps with variables are NOT in L(G)

formulas.name: CFL Closure Properties
formulas.latex: \text{CFLs closed under: } \cup, \cdot, {}^*, \text{homomorphism, inverse-hom, intersection with regular} \\  \text{NOT closed under: } \cap \text{ (two CFLs)}, \; \complement
formulas.plain: CFLs closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, intersection with regular. NOT closed under intersection of two CFLs or complement.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving or disproving that a given language is context-free using closure properties
formulas.trap: CFL intersection with a REGULAR language IS a CFL; intersection of TWO CFLs is NOT necessarily CFL

formulas.name: Pumping Lemma for CFLs
formulas.latex: \forall L \in \text{CFL}, \exists p: \forall w \in L, |w| \geq p \Rightarrow w = uvxyz \text{ with } |vy| \geq 1, |vxy| \leq p, \forall i \geq 0: uv^i xy^i z \in L
formulas.plain: Every CFL has pumping length p; strings longer than p split as uvxyz where |vy|>=1, |vxy|<=p, and pumping v and y keeps string in L
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a language is NOT context-free by contradicting the pumping lemma
formulas.trap: Both v and y are pumped simultaneously (not independently); |vy|>=1 ensures at least one is non-empty

formulas.name: Decidable Problems for CFG
formulas.latex: \text{Decidable: } w \in L(G)?\; (\text{CYK}),\; L(G)=\emptyset?,\; |L(G)|<\infty? \\ \text{Undecidable: } L(G_1)=L(G_2)?,\; \text{Ambiguous}(G)?,\; L(G)=\Sigma^*?
formulas.plain: Decidable: membership (CYK), emptiness, finiteness. Undecidable: equivalence of two CFGs, ambiguity, universality.
formulas.whenToUse: Any GATE question about which CFG problems are decidable vs undecidable

terms.means: Cocke-Younger-Kasami algorithm for membership in O(n^3)

formulas.trap: Ambiguity of a specific CFG is undecidable; but whether a string has TWO parse trees can be checked manually for small examples

formulas.name: Ambiguous Grammar
formulas.latex: G \text{ is ambiguous if } \exists w \in L(G) \text{ with two distinct leftmost derivations (or two parse trees)}
formulas.plain: A grammar is ambiguous if some string has two or more distinct parse trees (or two distinct leftmost derivations)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to determine if a grammar is ambiguous or to construct an unambiguous grammar

terms.means: always expand the leftmost non-terminal first

formulas.trap: Ambiguity is a property of the grammar, not the language; an ambiguous grammar may have an equivalent unambiguous grammar (inherent ambiguity is different)

formulas.name: Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)
formulas.latex: \text{Every production is } A \to BC \text{ or } A \to a \text{ (or } S \to \epsilon \text{ if } \epsilon \in L)
formulas.plain: CNF: every rule is A->BC (two variables) or A->a (single terminal). Every CFG can be converted to CNF.
formulas.whenToUse: Required for CYK algorithm and proving results about parse trees

terms.means: non-terminal variables (B, C not start symbol)

formulas.trap: In CNF, unit productions (A->B) and epsilon productions (A->epsilon) are eliminated except S->epsilon

formulas.name: PDA Formal Definition
formulas.latex: M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F) \\ \delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\epsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}
formulas.plain: PDA M = (Q, Sigma, Gamma, delta, q0, Z0, F): Q=states, Sigma=input alphabet, Gamma=stack alphabet, delta=transition, q0=start, Z0=initial stack symbol, F=accept states
formulas.whenToUse: Formally defining or analyzing a PDA
formulas.trap: PDA transition reads ONE input symbol (or epsilon) and ONE stack symbol, but can push a STRING onto the stack

formulas.name: PDA Acceptance Modes
formulas.latex: L(M)_{\text{final}} = \{ w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q_f, \epsilon, \gamma), q_f \in F \} \\ L(M)_{\text{empty}} = \{ w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q, \epsilon, \epsilon) \}
formulas.plain: Final-state acceptance: end in a final state. Empty-stack acceptance: end with empty stack. Both define the same class (CFL) for NPDA.
formulas.whenToUse: When converting between acceptance modes or checking which mode a PDA uses
formulas.trap: For NPDA, both acceptance modes are equivalent (same class). For DPDA, final-state acceptance is strictly more powerful than empty-stack

formulas.name: PDA and CFG Equivalence
formulas.latex: L \text{ is a CFL} \iff L \text{ is accepted by some NPDA}
formulas.plain: A language is context-free if and only if it is accepted by some non-deterministic pushdown automaton (NPDA).
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a language is CFL by constructing a PDA, or vice versa
formulas.trap: The equivalence holds for NPDA only; DPDA is strictly weaker than NPDA (not every CFL is accepted by a DPDA)

formulas.name: PDA Transition Notation
formulas.latex: \delta(q, a, A) = \{(p, \beta)\} \text{ means: in state } q, \text{ read } a, \text{ pop } A, \text{ push } \beta, \text{ go to state } p
formulas.plain: delta(q, a, A) = {(p, beta)}: in state q reading input 'a' with 'A' on top of stack, pop A, push beta (left-end first), move to state p
formulas.whenToUse: Reading or writing PDA transition tables/diagrams

terms.means: input symbol read (or epsilon for epsilon-transition)
string pushed onto stack (leftmost symbol becomes new top)

formulas.trap: Stack push is left-to-right: if beta=XY, X becomes the new top. Pushing epsilon = just popping A without pushing anything

formulas.name: DPDA Definition (Determinism Condition)
formulas.latex: \text{DPDA: for all } q \in Q, a \in \Sigma, A \in \Gamma: \\ |\delta(q, a, A)| + |\delta(q, \epsilon, A)| \leq 1
formulas.plain: DPDA: at most one transition for each (state, input-or-epsilon, stack-top) triple — no nondeterministic choices
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a PDA is deterministic
formulas.trap: A DPDA cannot have both a real-input transition AND an epsilon-transition from the same (state, stack-top) pair

formulas.name: DCFL Hierarchy
formulas.latex: \text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL} \subsetneq \text{CSL}
formulas.plain: Regular ⊂ DCFL ⊂ CFL ⊂ CSL (proper inclusions). Every regular language is a DCFL; every DCFL is a CFL; not every CFL is a DCFL.
formulas.whenToUse: Any GATE question about which automaton class accepts a given language

terms.means: languages accepted by NPDA = context-free languages

formulas.trap: {a^n b^n c^n} is NOT CFL (requires linear bounded automaton); {a^n b^n} IS DCFL; {ww} is NOT CFL; {ww^R} IS DCFL

formulas.name: DCFL Closure Properties
formulas.latex: \text{DCFLs closed under: } \complement \text{ (complement)} \\ \text{NOT closed under: } \cup, \cap, \cdot, {}^*
formulas.plain: DCFLs ARE closed under complement. DCFLs are NOT closed under union, intersection, concatenation, or Kleene star.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving or disproving that a language is a DCFL using closure properties

terms.means: complement — DCFL IS closed under this

formulas.trap: Unlike CFLs (NOT closed under complement), DCFLs ARE closed under complement — a classic GATE trap

formulas.name: DPDA: Final State vs Empty Stack
formulas.latex: L(\text{DPDA, final state}) \supsetneq L(\text{DPDA, empty stack}) \\ \text{Every regular language} \in L(\text{DPDA, final state})
formulas.plain: DPDA by final state accepts strictly more languages than DPDA by empty stack. Every regular language is accepted by DPDA (final state).
formulas.whenToUse: When acceptance mode of a DPDA affects what languages are accepted

terms.means: DCFL — languages accepted by DPDA with final states

formulas.trap: For NPDA both modes are equivalent; for DPDA they are NOT — final state is strictly more powerful

formulas.name: a^n b^n is DCFL
formulas.latex: L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\} \in \text{DCFL}
formulas.plain: L = { a^n b^n | n >= 0 } is a DCFL (push a's, pop on b's — deterministic)
formulas.whenToUse: Prototype DCFL; used as a benchmark for stack-matching problems

terms.means: equal number of a's and b's

formulas.trap: a^n b^n c^n is NOT CFL at all; a^n b^m with n != m is also CFL but not the same as a^n b^n

formulas.name: ww^R is DCFL (even palindromes)
formulas.latex: L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \Sigma^*\} \in \text{DCFL} \text{ (even-length palindromes)}
formulas.plain: L = { ww^R | w in {a,b}* } is a DCFL: push first half, then pop matching second half using a center marker or length
formulas.whenToUse: Palindrome-related PDA constructions; GATE frequently tests whether palindromes are DCFL
formulas.trap: Even-length palindromes (ww^R) are DCFL; odd-length palindromes and all palindromes over {a,b,c} are CFL but NOT DCFL

formulas.name: ww is NOT CFL
formulas.latex: L = \{ww \mid w \in \Sigma^*\} \notin \text{CFL} \text{ (provable by pumping lemma)}
formulas.plain: L = { ww | w in {a,b}* } is NOT context-free. Proof: apply pumping lemma — any pump breaks the two-copy structure.
formulas.whenToUse: Classic example of a language that is NOT CFL; used in pumping lemma proofs

terms.means: w concatenated with itself (same string twice)

formulas.trap: ww (same string twice) is NOT CFL but ww^R (string and its reverse) IS CFL and even DCFL

formulas.name: a^n b^n c^n is NOT CFL
formulas.latex: L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\} \notin \text{CFL}
formulas.plain: L = { a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0 } is NOT context-free. A single stack cannot enforce equality of three groups simultaneously.
formulas.whenToUse: Classic non-CFL example; pumping lemma proof target

terms.means: equal counts of three different symbols

formulas.trap: {a^n b^n c^n} is NOT accepted by any PDA; it requires a linear bounded automaton (CSL)

formulas.name: Matched Brackets / Balanced Parentheses
formulas.latex: L = \{w \mid w \text{ is balanced parentheses}\} \in \text{DCFL}
formulas.plain: Balanced parentheses / matched brackets is a DCFL: push open brackets, pop on matching close brackets.
formulas.whenToUse: Bracket-matching and expression parsing — archetypal DPDA application

terms.means: every open bracket has a matching close bracket in correct order

formulas.trap: Multiple types of brackets (like {[()]}) can still be handled deterministically with a single stack — still DCFL

formulas.name: PDA for a^n b^n (standard construction)
formulas.latex: \delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = (q_0, AZ_0) \\ \delta(q_0, a, A) = (q_0, AA) \\ \delta(q_0, b, A) = (q_1, \epsilon) \\ \delta(q_1, b, A) = (q_1, \epsilon) \\ \delta(q_1, \epsilon, Z_0) = (q_f, Z_0)
formulas.plain: PDA for a^n b^n: push A for each 'a', pop A for each 'b', accept when stack has only Z0 left
formulas.whenToUse: Template for building PDA that counts and matches two groups of symbols
formulas.trap: After reading all a's, switch to popping state; do not mix pushing and popping states

formulas.name: PDA for Even Palindromes ww^R
formulas.latex: \text{Push first half; use nondeterminism to guess midpoint; pop matching second half}
formulas.plain: PDA for ww^R: guess the midpoint nondeterministically, push first half onto stack, then pop while reading second half in reverse
formulas.whenToUse: Building NPDA for palindromes; also constructing DPDA for even-length palindromes with end-of-string marker

terms.means: nondeterministic transition to switch from push to pop phase

formulas.trap: For even-length palindromes, a DPDA can use a center marker or process until input is half-read. For odd palindromes or all palindromes, NPDA is needed.
